coredata.dc:title: Influence of the iodine content of nitrogen- and iodine-doped carbon dots as a peroxidase mimetic nanozyme exhibiting antifungal activity against C. albicans
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiao
Wu, Xiaomei
Yuan, Tao
Zhu, Junrun
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen- and iodine-doped carbon dots (I-CDs) were synthesized as peroxidase mimics for the antifungal disinfection of Candida albicans (C. albicans). The prepared I-CDs samples exhibited peroxidase-like activity in the presence of exogenous H2O2. The peroxidase-like activities and antifungal effects of the I-CDs samples were enhanced by increasing their iodine content from 0.1599 g to 0.4796 g. The antifungal activity of the I-CDs (i.e., 2.72 mg/mL) against C. albicans reached > 90% in the presence of exogenous H2O2 (0.5 mM) under visible light irradiation for 120 min. The in vitro experimental results showed the excellent photocatalytic antifungal activity of the designed system against C. albicans. It was theorized that this system promotes the formation of reactive oxygen species, including hydroxyl radicals (·OH), which can successfully inhibit the growth of C. albicans. The antifungal effect of the synergistic I-CDs/H2O2 system on C. albicans could be attributed to the combined effects of its peroxidase-like and photocatalytic activities; the increased activities appeared to be closely related to the iodine content. The results of the present study are expected to broaden the biological applications of the I-CDs and elucidate the relationship between the iodine content and peroxidase-like enzymatic and antifungal activities of the I-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen- and iodine-doped carbon dots
C. albicans
Peroxidase mimic
Nanozyme
Antifungal activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Peroxidase-like activity of the I-CDs samples
Antifungal activity of the I-CDs samples
Possible mechanism of the antifungal activity of I-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Talent Development Program of Yunnan province
Basic research program of Yunnan province
Open Project of Yunnan Provincial Reproductive and Obstetrics and Gynecology Clinical Medicine Center

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Basic research program of Yunnan province (2018FE117,2019FE174,202001AY070001-115), the Open Project of Yunnan Provincial Reproductive and Obstetrics and Gynecology Clinical Medicine Center (2019LCZXF-SZ003), and the Talent Development Program of Yunnan province (No. D-2018054).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS spectra of I-CDs-3: (a) C 1s, (b) O 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) I 3d. Fluorescence spectra of I-CDs-3: (e) Excitation and emission spectra of I-CDs-3; (f) emission intensities of I-CDs-3 at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) UV–visible absorbance spectra of the oxidized TMB in the different reaction systems: (a) TMB+H2O2; (b) TMB+H2O2+I-CDs-1; (c) TMB +H2O2+I-CDs-2; and (d) TMB+H2O2+I-CDs-3. Inset shows the color changes in TMB in the different reaction systems. (b) Antifungal effects of the different I-CDs toward C. albicans in the presence of H2O2 (0.5 mM) under light and dark conditions.
Steady-state kinetic curves for I-CDs-3: (a) H2O2 and (c) TMB. Lineweaver–Burk plots for I-CDs-3: (b) H2O2, and (d) TMB.

(a) Antifungal effect of I-CDs-3 on C. albicans under light and dark conditions. (b) Survival rate of C. albicans following incubation under light and dark conditions at different I-CDs-3 concentrations. (c) Time-dependent inhibition efficiency of I-CDs-3 (2.72 mg/mL) treated with H2O2 (0.5 mM) under light and dark conditions.

SEM images of C. albicans treated with 0.5 mM H2O2 and/or 2.72 mg/mL I-CDs-3 under visible light irradiation for 120 min (a) Blank control; (b) treated with I-CDs-3; (c) treated with H2O2; and (d) treated with I-CDs-3+H2O2.

(a) Histograms of the reduction rates of the I-CDs-3+TMB+H2O2 reaction in the presence of different scavengers under visible light irradiation. (b) Fluorescence spectra (Ex = 342 nm) of the solutions containing only BA, BA+H2O2, only I-CDs-3, BA+I-CDs-3, and BA+I-CDs-3+H2O2.
Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic inactivation of C. albicans by I-CDs-3.

head.ce:title: Influence of the iodine content of nitrogen- and iodine-doped carbon dots as a peroxidase mimetic nanozyme exhibiting antifungal activity against C. albicans

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Gynecology, Affiliated Hospital of Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650000, Yunnan Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Gynecology, Affiliated Hospital of Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of gynecology, Affiliated Hospital of Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650000, Yunnan Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen- and iodine-doped carbon dots (I-CDs) were synthesized as peroxidase mimics for the antifungal disinfection of Candida albicans (C. albicans). The prepared I-CDs samples exhibited peroxidase-like activity in the presence of exogenous H2O2. The peroxidase-like activities and antifungal effects of the I-CDs samples were enhanced by increasing their iodine content from 0.1599 g to 0.4796 g. The antifungal activity of the I-CDs (i.e., 2.72 mg/mL) against C. albicans reached > 90% in the presence of exogenous H2O2 (0.5 mM) under visible light irradiation for 120 min. The in vitro experimental results showed the excellent photocatalytic antifungal activity of the designed system against C. albicans. It was theorized that this system promotes the formation of reactive oxygen species, including hydroxyl radicals (·OH), which can successfully inhibit the growth of C. albicans. The antifungal effect of the synergistic I-CDs/H2O2 system on C. albicans could be attributed to the combined effects of its peroxidase-like and photocatalytic activities; the increased activities appeared to be closely related to the iodine content. The results of the present study are expected to broaden the biological applications of the I-CDs and elucidate the relationship between the iodine content and peroxidase-like enzymatic and antifungal activities of the I-CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) is an infection of the vaginal mucosa and/or vulva caused by Candida albicans (C. albicans) [1], and it is estimated that approximately 75% of women suffer at least one episode of VVC in their lifetime [2]. Because of the high frequency of acute or chronic relapses, VVC can have a profound impact on a woman's quality of life [3]. In most studies carried out to date, it has been reported that more than 90% of the acute sporadic VVC episodes worldwide are caused by C. albicans [4,5]. The most commonly used antifungal drugs for its treatment are azole, polyene, echinocandins, pyrimidines, and antifungal agents, although some studies have shown that C. albicans shows varying degrees of resistance to these drugs [6]. The production of H2O2 by the vaginal lactobacilli is one of the most critical defense mechanisms against C. albicans [7]. However, the low physiological concentrations of H2O2 (< 100 μM) fail to inactivate such pathogens in the vagina. It was found that upon increasing the concentration of H2O2 to more than 10 mM, the toxicity towards lactobacillus vaginalis was increased, although an obvious bacteriostatic effect was also observed [8]. As the number of patients increase every day and given that this fungal disease has a high prevalence in larger populations, it is important to find a safe and proper treatment [9] .

Nanozymes are nanomaterials that can catalyze the reactions taking place in biological systems [10,11]. More specifically, they can catalyze the reactions of biological substrates, with a higher tolerance towards harsh reaction conditions, and have great potential and application prospects as new antimicrobial agents [12–14]. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are a series of molecules [15,16] including H2O2, singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide ions (O2·−), and hydroxyl radicals (·OH), that have been demonstrated to effectively inhibit bacterial growth [17]. It has been shown that the antibacterial activity exhibited by nanozymes can be mainly attributed to their peroxidation activity, which promotes the production of hydroxyl radicals (·OH) from exogenous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and increases the level of intracellular ROS production [18,19].

Although the catalytic production of ·OH from H2O2 can be used as a strategy against bacteria [20,21], nanozymes have been shown to exhibit low antibacterial effects along with potential side effects [22]. Similarly, the low efficiency, slow reaction process, and high effective concentration of H2O2 limit the further applications of such systems [23]. Moreover, due to complex preparation processes and high costs, synthesizing antifungal nanomaterials with high antibacterial activities and strong selectivities still remains a formidable challenge [24,25]. Indeed, only a few reports currently exist on nanozymes that exhibit both high antifungal activities and good biocompatibilities.

In this study, nitrogen- and iodine-doped carbon dots (I-CDs) were prepared as nanozymes to mimic the activity of peroxidase. The relationship between the peroxidase-like and antifungal activities of the I-CDs was confirmed by a group of in vitro experiments. Furthermore, the effects of various experimental parameters on the catalytic and antifungal activities of the I-CDs were also elucidated. The influence of the iodine content on the catalytic activity was then evaluated through a series of experiments to identify the possible antifungal mechanism of the prepared systems. The obtained results and the ascertained enzymatic activities of the prepared systems will widen the application base of I-CDs in biological sciences and contribute towards gaining a deeper understanding of the relationship between the iodine content and peroxidase-like enzymatic and antifungal activities of these synthesized materials.

Iopromide (C18H24I3N3O8), 3-iodo-L-tyrosine (C9H10INO3), 3,5-diiodo-DL-tyrosine (C9H9I2NO3), and sodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) were purchased from J&K Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), citric acid (C6H8O7), 3,3',5,5'-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), 2′,7′-dichloro-fluoresceindiacetate (DCFH-DA), isopropanol (IPA), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and 4-hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-N-oxyl (TEMPOL) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China)·H2O2 solution (30%) was obtained from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China).

The C. albicans strains, used for the antifungal assays, and HT29, used for the cytotoxicity assays, were cultured specimens collected at the Kunming University of Science and Technology and stored at − 4 ℃. For the antifungal assays, Sabouraud dextrose (SD) broth and Sabouraud dextrose agar (SDA) were obtained from Beijing Land Bridge Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China) and stored at 25 ℃. The RPMI-1640 culture medium, used for the cytotoxicity assays, was purchased from Procell Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China) and stored at − 4 ℃.

In this study, three kinds of nitrogen- and iodine-doped carbon dots were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Initially, 3-iodo-L-tyrosine (0.387 g, 1.264 mmol), 3,5-diiodo-DL-tyrosine (0.546 g, 1.264 mmol), and iopromide (1 g, 1.264 mmol) were diluted separately in ultrapure water (100 mL) containing ethylenediamine (100 μL, 1.498 mmol). The three mixtures were then placed in high-pressure Teflon-lined vessels and allowed to react at 180 °C for 10 h. Afterward, each mixture was naturally cooled to 25 ℃, and then centrifuged at 1509g for 20 min to remove the large particles (Sorvall ST1 Plus, Thermofisher sientific Co., Ltd. Shanghai, China), yielding a supernatant liquid. The supernatant liquid was subsequently filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane filter (Xingya Technology Co., Ltd. Beijing, Shanghai China), and the resulting I-CD solutions were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C prior to further use. The CDs prepared using 3-iodo-L-tyrosine, 3,5-diiodo-DL-tyrosine, and iopromide were labeled as I-CDs-1, I-CDs-2, and I-CDs-3, respectively.

A drop of the aqueous suspension of nanoparticles was added onto a piece of the carbon-coated copper grid, dried under ambient conditions, and then viewed under a transmission electron microscope (TEM; FEI Tecnai G2 F30 microscope, FEI, USA) to examine the nanoparticle morphology [26]. Following freeze-drying of I-CDs-3 to obtain fine solid particles, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Kratos, UK) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, TENSOR27 FTIR spectrometer, Bruker, Germany) were carried out to identify the chemical state of its surface constituents [27]. UV–visible spectrophotometry (UV-2550, Hitachi, Shimadzu, Japan) was used for the enzymatic studies, and a G9800A Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) was used to acquire the fluorescence spectra. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (S-4800 FESEM, Hitachi, Japan) was used to investigate the morphology and structural integrity [28] of different groups of C. albicans. The zeta potentials were determined using a Horiba SZ-100 nanoparticle analyzer (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan) at 25 °C.

The catalytic oxidation of TMB was measured in the presence of H2O2 to investigate the peroxidase-like activity of I-CDs-1, I-CDs-2, and I-CDs-3. In this process, an aliquot (200 μL) of the I-CDs (I-CDs-1, I-CDs-2, or I-CDs-3) was dispersed in an acetate buffer (2400 μL, 0.2 M, pH 4.0), and a 6 mM solution of TMB (200 μL) was added into it along with a 30 mM solution of H2O2 (200 μL). The final solution was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. All of the reaction mixtures were monitored by a UV–visible spectrophotometer. I-CDs-3 was selected for the subsequent experiments owing to its optimum peroxide-like activity. The effects of pH (2.0–6.0) and temperature (20–70 °C) on the catalytic activity of I-CDs-3 were also studied (Fig. S3). The relative activity (%) in each group of experiments was defined as the ratio of activity to maximum activity.

The strain preservation of C. albicans was performed using SD broth. A single colony of the fungi was cultured in SD broth (5 mL) under continuous shaking at 190 rpm and 37 ºC, over 24 h, and then diluted to approximately 1 × 107 CFU/mL for use in the experiments.

The antifungal efficiencies of I-CDs-1, I-CDs-2, and I-CDs-3 against C. albicans were determined according to the following experimental procedure: Initially, the fungal suspension (5 mL) was mixed with the desired concentration of an as-prepared H2O2 solution. To perform the photocatalytic disinfection of C. albicans, the prepared C. albicans samples, both with and without the I-CDs, were irradiated for 120 min by a light-emitting diode (16 W) emitting visible light. This process was also performed under dark conditions for the control group samples. Subsequently, an aliquot (100 μL) of the as-prepared sample solution was spread onto the SDA plates. The number of colony forming units was counted and recorded after incubation at 37 ℃ for 48 h. The antifungal efficiency was calculated using Eq. (1) [29], expressed as:
(1)
Antifungal
efficiency
%
=
N
control
−
N
sample
/
N
control
* 100
%
where
N
control
is the number of colonies in the control sample, and
N
sample
is the number of colonies remaining after the treatment with I-CDs.

The cytotoxicity assay was performed using the MTT, which was metabolically reduced in the viable cells to form a blue-violet insoluble formazan. The number of viable cells is correlated to the color intensity, which was determined via photometric measurements after dissolving the formazan in DMSO. Initially, HT29 cells were cultured in 96-well plates at a density of 10,000 cells/well to test the biocompatibility of I-CDs-3. After a 24 h incubation, the cell viabilities resulting from the application of different I-CDs-3 concentrations (0–300 μg/mL) were observed using the MTT assay [30,31].

I-CDs-3 was selected for performing the characterizations because of its superior peroxidase activity. The TEM observations (Fig. S2a) showed that I-CDs-3 was spherical with a well-dispersed particle size distribution between 2 and 5 nm. The corresponding high-resolution TEM image showed that the synthesized I-CDs-3 exhibited crystal structures with good lattice spacing resolutions.

The FTIR spectrum revealed the surface functional groups of I-CDs-3 (Fig. S2b). The peak at 2935 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of the C–H groups, whereas that at 3420 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of the O–H functional groups. These observed peaks confirmed the presence of these functional groups on the surface of the as-synthesized I-CDs-3. In addition, C–C, C–N, and CO stretching vibrations were observed at 1250, 1400, and 1635 cm−1, respectively, and the bands at 490 and 1100 cm−1 were ascribed to the C–I and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively.

The XPS results of I-CDs-3 were highly consistent with those of FTIR and provided further evidence for identifying the surface functional groups of I-CDs-3. Fig. 1a shows the C 1s spectra, where the main peaks are at 288.0 eV (CO), 286.0 eV (C–O/C–N), and 284.8 eV (C–C). As illustrated in Fig. 1b, the O 1s spectra contained two major peaks corresponding to the CO (531.5 eV) and C–O/OH (533.0 eV) moieties. Furthermore, the two deconvoluted peaks shown in the N 1s XPS spectra in Fig. 1c are centered at 400.0 eV (C–NH2) and 402.0 eV (N–I/CO–NH). Moreover, the I 3d spectra confirmed the presence of C–I (618.0 eV) and N–I (620.5 eV) (Fig. 1d) in I-CDs-3, thereby confirming that the synthetic I-CDs-3 samples were successfully doped with iodine species during their preparation [32].

Fig. 1e shows the fluorescence spectra of I-CDs-3, wherein the excitation and emission peak maxima are observed at 416 and 502 nm, respectively. In addition, the emission spectra of the prepared I-CDs-3 were obtained at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1f), and the results confirmed that the emission was purely based on the excitation wavelength; this dependency was possibly caused by the sample particle sizes and surface state distributions [33].

Surface charge is an important variable that is typically defined for studying the binding process during the interaction between any compound and bacteria [34]. Various studies have shown that for many novel antimicrobial compounds, such as those containing nanoparticles, their affinity towards lipid membranes is related to their antibacterial activity [35]. The zeta potential measurement method is a powerful technique for evaluating the interactions of such antimicrobial compounds with bacteria. In our experiment, the zeta potentials of I-CDs-1, I-CDs-2, I-CDs-3 were found to be − 20.24 mV, − 5.99 mV, and − 5.46 mV, respectively (Fig. S6). Most bacteria exhibit a negative zeta potential when the pH is higher than 2 [36,37]. These results imply that I-CDs-3 is more likely to get attached to the C. albicans surfaces owing to its lower zeta potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like activity of the I-CDs samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Previous studies have shown that carbon-based materials can exhibit enzymatic activities, as a result of nitrogen doping [38,39]. Thus, the peroxidase-mimicking properties of the prepared I-CDs were evaluated using TMB as a substrate [40]. The enzymatic reaction taking place between TMB and H2O2 in the presence of a peroxidase is shown in Eq. 2 below:
(2)
H
2
O
2
+ TMB
→
peroxidase
TMB
2 +
+ 2
H
2
O

As illustrated in Fig. 2a (a), no absorbance was observed in the absence of the I-CDs, i.e. the absorbance was almost zero, and the color of TMB remained relatively changed. When I-CDs-1 was added to the reaction system, the solution did not turn blue (Fig. 2a (b)) because TMB was not oxidized. Conversely, the oxidized TMB, upon the addition of I-CDs-2 in the presence of H2O2, showed a relatively higher absorbance at 652 nm (Fig. 2a (c)). However, I-CDs-3, in the presence of H2O2, catalyzed the oxidation of TMB to produce a blue product and yielded the maximum absorbance, among all the aforementioned cases, at 652 nm (Fig. 2a (d)). These results indicate that the catalytic activities of the I-CDs increased with increasing iodine content (i.e., I-CDs-1: 0.1599 g; I-CDs-2: 0.3197 g; I-CDs-3: 0.4796 g). As a result, I-CDs-3 was selected for the subsequent experiments owing to its superior peroxidase-like activity.

Because temperature and pH are known to have a significant influence on the biological and chemical systems [41], the peroxidase-like behavior of I-CDs-3 was evaluated under different temperature and pH conditions. As shown in Fig. S3, I-CDs-3 maintained a peroxidase activity > 70% over a pH range of 3.0–4.5, at the physiological temperature (i.e., 37 °C). The peroxidase-like activity of I-CDs-3 remained above 70% over the normal vaginal pH range (mildly acidic vaginal pH around 4.5) [42]. Notably, I-CDs-3 exhibited a high activity over a broad temperature range, showing less sensitivity to temperature than the horseradish peroxidase (HRP) does [43]. These results suggest that I-CDs-3 is a promising antifungal agent under physiological conditions.

The steady-state kinetic parameters of the reaction between I-CDs-3 and TMB were studied to evaluate the peroxidase-like activity of I-CDs-3 ( Fig. 3). It was found that the TMB oxidation reaction, catalyzed by I-CDs-3, was dependent on the substrate, i.e., TMB, concentration, and a corresponding typical Michaelis–Menten curve was obtained within a specific H2O2 or TMB concentration range. The Lineweaver–Burk plot was used to calculate the Michaelis–Menten constant (K
m ) [44]. The K
m values of I-CDs-3 were 0.097 mM for H2O2 and 0.042 mM for TMB, and the maximum reaction rates, i.e., V
max values were 27.86 × 10−8 M/s for H2O2 and 16.10 × 10−8 M/s for TMB.

In addition, these K
m and V
max values were compared with those of the natural HRP and other reported inorganic materials, as shown in Table 1. It was observed that a lower K
m resulted in higher affinity of the enzyme to the substrate, thereby indicating a higher catalytic efficiency. As can be seen in Table 1 , the K
m value of I-CDs-3 is lower than that of HRP, indicating a higher affinity [45]. Compared to HRP, I-CDs-3 possessed a larger number of active sites and a larger specific surface area [46]. Therefore, owing to its excellent peroxidase-like activity and convenient preparation route, I-CDs-3 presents broad application prospects in the fields of biomedical and environmental chemistry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifungal activity of the I-CDs samples
ce:section.ce:para: The antifungal activities of the I-CDs samples against C. albicans were examined by plating and counting the number of viable colonies. Initially, C. albicans was treated with the same concentrations of I-CDs and H2O2 under visible light for the same period of time. The results showed that I-CDs-3 exhibited the best peroxidase-like activity and antifungal effect in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 2b). These results were in good agreement with the UV absorption spectra (Fig. 2a). For C. albicans, the treatment with I-CDs-1+H2O2 or H2O2 alone can reduce its fungal activity by only a small amount. However, with the assistance of I-CDs-2 and I-CDs-3, H2O2 dramatically reduced the survival rates of C. albicans. Especially, I-CDs-3 could potentiate the antifungal activity of H2O2 in a better way than I-CDs-2 could. These results showed that the peroxidase-like activities of the I-CDs samples were positively correlated with their antifungal activities.

The photocatalytic antifungal activity of I-CDs-3 against C. albicans was observed by adding different concentrations of I-CDs-3 (i.e., 0.68, 1.36, 2.04, and 2.72 mg/mL), at different time intervals (i.e., 15, 30, 60, 90, and 120 min). The results are shown in Fig. 4. Compared to the H2O2 control group, the treatment of C. albicans with H2O2 and I-CDs-3 mixture resulted in a potent concentration-dependent antifungal activity. The control group (0.5 mM H2O2) exhibited only modest antifungal effects, with more than 80% of the C. albicans cells surviving under both light and dark conditions. In the presence of I-CDs-3, the inhibition effect was significantly improved, and when the concentration of I-CDs-3 was increased to 2.04 mg/mL, more than 90% of the C. albicans cells were killed under the light conditions. In addition, at a concentration of 2.72 mg/mL, the combination of I-CDs-3 with H2O2 almost completely inhibited the growth of C. albicans after visible light treatment for 120 min. These results indicate that the synergistic antifungal effect of I-CDs-3 and H2O2 was enhanced by increasing the I-CDs-3 concentration, and visible light irradiation further enhanced this effect. In terms of biological systems, the concentration of H2O2 produced by the vaginal lactobacilli strain was previously reported to be 3.53 ± 0.15 mM [51]. Notably, we found that the synthesized I-CDs-3 could exert a strong inhibitory effect on C. albicans under lower H2O2 concentrations than those found in the vagina.

SEM was used to investigate the morphological changes induced by the antifungal system, wherein C. albicans was observed before and after the I-CDs-3/H2O2 treatment. As shown in Fig. 5a, the untreated C. albicans exhibited a typical structure, with a smooth and continuous cell surface. Similarly, the cell walls of C. albicans, treated only with I-CDs-3 (Fig. 5b), were intact and smooth, with the cells displaying their typical shape. However, after treatment with only H2O2 (Fig. 5c), the fungal cell surface became rough, and when the cells were treated with both I-CDs-3 and H2O2 (Fig. 5d), they became rough, corrugated, and flattened. These changes became more prominent upon increasing the I-CDs-3 concentration, which increasingly destroyed the C. albicans cells. Consequently, more pits were observed in the cell walls, indicating a stronger antibacterial activity of I-CDs-3+H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the antifungal activity of I-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
It has been reported that ·OH, O2·−, and holes (h+) destroy the bacterial cell membrane [52,53]; however, C. albicans and other fungi are different from bacteria in their composition, biological behavior, drug sensitivity, and drug resistance mechanisms. Therefore, it was theorized that the simulated catalytic activity of the nanoenzymes could effectively inhibit the growth of C. albicans through the ROS mechanism. DCFH-DA was used as a fluorescence probe to examine the enhancement in the ROS production in the reaction systems [54]. As can be seen in Fig. S7, the H2O2+I-CDs-1 treatment showed negligible fluorescence enhancement, whereas the H2O2+I-CDs-2 treatment produced a weak fluorescence signal. Notably, in the H2O2+I-CDs-3 group, the production of ROS reached its highest value at E
m = 524 nm. Thus, I-CDS-3, in the presence of H2O2, increased the fluorescence intensity of DCFH through the decomposition of H2O2 into •OH.

Trapping experiments were therefore carried out to detect the important active species, using TEMPOL as an 1O2 and O2·− scavenger [55], isopropanol as a h+ scavenger [56], and DMSO as an ·OH scavenger [57]. The absorbances of the different TMB mixtures were measured at a wavelength of 652 nm to identify the radicals that can exhibit antifungal activity. Thus, UV–visible spectroscopy of the samples (i.e., TMB+H2O2+I-CDs-3), containing the different scavengers, was carried out, and the corresponding results are presented in Fig. S4a. It was found that upon the addition of DMSO, isopropanol, and TEMPOL, the UV–visible absorbances decreased to 60.51%, 79.39%, and 76.82%, respectively ( Fig. 6a), indicating that ·OH played a leading role in the catalytic reaction. As can be seen in Fig. S5, the synthesized I-CDs-3 could catalyze the conversion of H2O2 into ·OH. Subsequently, benzoic acid reacted with ·OH to produce a highly fluorescent product, namely hydroxybenzoic acid [58]. It was found that in the presence of both I-CDs-3 and H2O2, the fluorescence intensity was significantly stronger than that in the presence of BA alone or BA+H2O2, thereby confirming the generation of ·OH and the peroxidase-like property of I-CDs-3 (Fig. 6b). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture of I-CDs-3 and H2O2 under the visible light conditions was higher than that under the dark conditions (Fig. S4b). This indicates that the catalytic activity of I-CDs-3 under visible light was stronger than that in the dark.

As can be seen in Fig. 7, the I-CD-3+H2O2 synergistic system produces e− (i.e., electrons) and h+ (i.e., hole) pairs under visible light conditions, and the generated electrons are effectively transferred from the conduction band to the valence band. Simultaneously, the photogenerated e− and h+ convert O2 and H2O to O2·− and ·OH, respectively. It has been reported that iodine-doped modification can narrow the bandgap and widen the light absorption range [59], and as a result, the transfer of photoinduced electrons can be facilitated [60]; this accelerates the formation of hydroxyl radicals from H2O2 [61]. As mentioned previously, the iodine contents of I-CDs-1, I-CDs-2, and I-CDs-3 corresponded to 0.1599, 0.3197, and 0.4796 g, and the peroxidase-like photocatalytic activity increased along with the increasing iodine content. It has been reported that the electron withdrawing groups significantly enhance the mimicking catalytic activity [62]. The aromatic compounds used in this study, i.e., C9H10INO3, C9H9I2NO3, and C18H24I3N3O8, contained one, two, and three aromatic carbon–iodine (–I) bonds, respectively. The Hammett σm value of the substituted organic linkers with −I was + 0.353 [63]. Consequently, it is speculated that the enhanced photocatalytic inactivation of C. albicans was caused by a gradual enhancement in the peroxidase-like activity, resulting from the increased iodine content and number of aromatic carbon–iodine bonds.

An efficacious antifungal agent must be able to affect fungi without influencing the host cells. Thus, to demonstrate the potential applications of I-CDs-3 in biomedicine, its stability under physiological conditions and biocompatibility towards mammol/Lalian cells were examined [64]. After observation for 60 d, the as-synthesized I-CDs-3 continued to exhibit a good peroxidase-like activity, with no significant decrease in its enzyme activity, thereby indicating that I-CDs-3 shows long-term stability in the physiological environment. Furthermore, it was found that the HT29 cell viability remained more than 80% after a 24 h treatment, even when the concentration of I-CDs-3 was increased to 300 μg/mL. These results were similar to those reported in the literature using synthetic nitrogen-doped solid carbon spheres with a concentration of 0–250 μg/mL [54]. These results, therefore, confirm the good biocompatibility of I-CDs-3.

In this study, an antifungal system combining nitrogen- and iodine-doped carbon dots (I-CDs) with a low dose of H2O2 was synthesized. It was found that the synthesized carbon dots exerted a strong inhibitory effect on C. albicans in the presence of H2O2 with a concentration lower than that present in the vagina. The possible mechanism of antifungal action was attributed to the formation of ROS, in which ·OH played a major role. The antifungal effects of the synergistic system of I-CDs and H2O2 on C. albicans may be attributed to the combined effects of the peroxidase-like and photocatalytic activities. Moreover, the increased peroxidase-like and photocatalytic activities appeared to be closely related to the iodine content, with higher activities being observed in the presence of higher iodine loadings. These results, therefore, indicate that the prepared I-CDs exhibit potential for application as antifungal agents for the treatment of fungal infections. In the future, we will design nanozymes with inhibition and killing effects on C. albicans and other microorganisms and study the relevant mechanisms.

Xiao Li: Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft. Xiaomei Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Tao Yuan: Resources, Visualization. Junrun Zhu: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yaling Yang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

This work was supported by the Basic research program of Yunnan province, China (2018FE117, 2019FE174, 202001AY070001-115), the Open Project of Yunnan Provincial Reproductive and Obstetrics and Gynecology Clinical Medicine Center, China (2019LCZXF-SZ003), and the Talent Development Program of Yunnan province, China (No. D-2018054).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
